blocks.html:
Because the API is stateless, every request is a fresh decision: what does the model need to see right now? Context management is that decision, made systematically. Send everything forever and sessions get slow, expensive, and unfocused; trim carelessly and the assistant forgets the user's allergy.

The key insight is that conversation content isn't one kind of thing. Safety-critical exact facts (allergies, dosages), revisable preferences (budget, dietary choice), and disposable chit-chat need different treatments: a protected reference section that never gets summarized, a structured state object updated on every revision, and summarization with recent turns kept verbatim. Applying one policy to all three is how exact facts get blurred away.

A big context window doesn't save you from this work — capacity is not attention. Fitting in the window doesn't make every detail equally salient; stale and verbose content dilutes what matters.

A meal-planning session, forty turns in: allergies stated early, "make everything vegetarian" stated in the middle, timing chat everywhere. Doubling the sliding window from 20 to 40 turns just defers the failure. The robust design: allergies and serving counts live in a retained reference section; current preferences live in a state object updated on each revision ("single source of current truth" — far more reliable than a "prioritize recent statements" instruction); the general discussion gets a structured summary (Decisions / Preferences / Open questions / Facts — never vague prose); the last few turns stay verbatim. And when a user states genuinely conflicting goals, surface the contradiction — don't silently average.

Patterns for specific traffic shapes:

  • Sliding window is right when logs show old turns are rarely referenced; also apply one specifically to accumulated RAG results (keep the last 2–3 retrievals).
  • Precision after summarization: when users ask for exact numbers your summaries blurred, re-inject relevant source sections on demand — lighter than a full fact store, tighter than "higher-fidelity summaries."
  • Tool result compression: keep task-relevant fields, drop 40-field payloads.
  • API-native mechanisms: compaction summarizes old history server-side; context editing prunes stale tool results. Great for "keep the session alive"; application-level structures still own must-survive-verbatim facts. The model_context_window_exceeded stop reason is your trigger to compact — never retry unchanged.
  • Returning users: start fresh with a structured summary + fresh lookups. Agents cite stale tool results despite instructions not to.
  • External updates mid-chat: inject fresh state into the next request, clearly outranking older tool results.

items: Different content kinds need different retention: reference section, state object, summary, verbatim recent turns.
Structured state beats 'prioritize recent preferences' instructions every time.
Summaries must extract decisions and facts — vague narrative loses what users ask about.
Compaction summarizes, context editing prunes; app-level structures guard verbatim facts.
Stale tool results are landmines: returning sessions get summaries + fresh lookups.
